unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Get ready to move! In this unit you learn the names of the parts of your body, and lots of action words such as hop, jump, clap and wave. You follow movement instructions and then give commands of your own. You also learn how to stay healthy and strong by moving every day, eating good food, drinking water and sleeping well. By the end you can say what someone is doing right now, just like Amal and her friends in this unit's story, “The Big Race”.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and do the action as you say each word.
Work through the six grammar practices: giving commands, actions happening now, and the -ing spelling rules.
Listen to the poem, the healthy living text, the “Get Up and Move” listening text and the story “The Big Race”, and answer the eighteen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 3 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon children on a school field hopping, clapping, waving and stretching, with body parts labelled.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The top part of your body, with your eyes, ears, nose and mouth.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wear a sun hat on my head outside.
practiceSentences: Amal puts a warm hat on her head before school.
Can you balance a small beanbag on your head?
“Turn your head and look at the board,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I shake my head from side to side to say no.
My head feels hot after I run around the field.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper, point to your head, and ask the tutor to check the sentence you say about it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The long part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I use my arms to carry my school bag.
practiceSentences: Leo swings his arms when he walks to the park.
How high can you lift both of your arms?
“Hold the box with two arms,” said Hana.
My right arm feels strong after I carry the water bottle.
We stretch our arms wide like the wings of a bird.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, describe one thing you do with your arms and ask the tutor if your sentence is clear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part at the end of your arm, with a thumb and fingers.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wash my hands before I eat my lunch.
practiceSentences: Maya claps her hands when the song begins.
Which hand do you write with at school?
“Put up your hand if you know it,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I hold my little sister's hand when we cross the road.
A hand has five fingers, and one is a thumb.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to watch while you show your hands, then ask the tutor to help you spell the word hand.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: One of the five long parts at the end of your hand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I hold my pencil with my fingers.
practiceSentences: Sami points at the map with one finger.
How many fingers can you wiggle at the same time?
“Put your finger on your nose,” said Nora, laughing.
I count to ten on my fingers in maths.
My finger has a small plaster on it today.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - i - n - g - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper nearby, count on your fingers out loud and ask the tutor to say each number back to you.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The front part of your body below your chest, also called the stomach.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My tummy feels full after a big lunch.
practiceSentences: Mina rubs her tummy when she is hungry.
Does your tummy rumble before breakfast?
“Put your hands on your tummy,” said Doctor Sarah.
I felt butterflies in my tummy before the race.
We watch our tummy go up and down when we breathe.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, then ask the tutor which other word means the same as tummy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: One of the five small parts at the end of your foot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can wiggle my toes inside my shoes.
practiceSentences: Theo hides his toes under the warm blanket.
Can you touch your toes without bending your knees?
“Stand on your toes and reach up high,” said Teacher Yasmin.
My toes feel cold on the tiles in the morning.
Ten toes help us balance when we stand on one foot.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a grown-up beside you, wiggle your toes and ask the tutor to check the sentence you make about them.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move your hand from side to side to say hello or goodbye.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wave goodbye to my friend at the school gate.
practiceSentences: Daniel waves to his grandmother from the bus window.
Will you wave when the runners pass our seats?
“Wave your arms above your head,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I wave with both hands when I am very happy.
The children wave small flags at the sports day parade.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper to stand across the room, wave to them, then ask the tutor to explain when people wave.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To jump up and down on one foot.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The children hop like little rabbits in the garden.
practiceSentences: Leo can hop all the way across the playground.
How many times can you hop on your left foot?
“Hop three times and then stop,” said Nora.
I hop over the puddle so my shoes stay dry.
Small birds hop along the wall to look for crumbs.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult watching, hop three times and ask the tutor how hopping is different from jumping.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To push yourself off the ground with both feet.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We jump over the puddles when it rains.
practiceSentences: Amal jumps high to catch the soft ball.
Can you jump and clap your hands at the same time?
“Jump up, then land gently,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I jump for joy when my team wins the game.
Frogs jump from the grass into the cool pond.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to count while you jump five times, then ask the tutor to check your sentence about jumping.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To hit your two hands together to make a sound.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We clap our hands at the end of the song.
practiceSentences: Mina claps loudly when her friend finishes the race.
Can you clap in time with the drum?
“Clap twice if you can hear me,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I clap my hands to keep the beat while we sing.
The whole class claps for every runner on sports day.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper nearby, clap a short pattern and ask the tutor to describe the sound you made.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move something with small, quick side-to-side movements.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I wiggle my toes in the warm sand.
practiceSentences: Sami wiggles his fingers to make a shadow puppet.
Can you wiggle your nose without using your hands?
“Wiggle your fingers like falling rain,” said Nora.
I wiggle in my chair when the story gets exciting.
Puppies wiggle their tails when they are pleased to see us.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - i - g - g - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to join in, wiggle your fingers together, then ask the tutor for two more wiggle sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move your head up and down to show that you agree.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I nod my head when I understand the question.
practiceSentences: Theo nods when his friend asks to share the ball.
Will you nod if you are ready to begin?
“Nod your head slowly, then keep it still,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I nod to my teacher instead of calling out.
People nod in many countries to show that they agree.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a grown-up beside you, nod your head and ask the tutor what nodding tells other people.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Moving your body on purpose to keep it fit and strong.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Running in the park is good exercise.
practiceSentences: Amal does ten minutes of exercise before breakfast.
What is your favourite exercise at school?
“Let us start with a gentle exercise,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I exercise with my brother Adam every Saturday morning.
Swimming is exercise that uses your arms and your legs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - e - r - c - i - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to time you, do one minute of exercise, then ask the tutor to name two more exercises.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Strong and well, and not feeling ill.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Fruit and vegetables help me stay healthy.
practiceSentences: Nora packs a healthy lunch with rice and beans.
Is a bowl of soup a healthy dinner?
“You look healthy and full of energy today,” said Doctor Sarah.
I feel healthy when I sleep well and drink water.
Clean air and green trees keep our town healthy too.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - a - l - t - h - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper, name three healthy foods and ask the tutor which one gives you the most energy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having lots of power in your body or your muscles.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Moving every day makes my muscles strong.
practiceSentences: Adam is strong enough to carry two heavy bags.
Which animal do you think is the strongest?
“Stand tall and make your legs feel strong,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I feel strong after I climb the ladder at the park.
Strong bones grow when we eat well and move often.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - r - o - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, name one thing that makes your body strong and ask the tutor to add another.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The clear drink that your body needs every single day.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I drink water when I feel thirsty.
practiceSentences: Leo fills his bottle with cold water before the race.
How many cups of water do you drink each day?
“Drink your water before we begin,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I keep a water bottle in my school bag.
Plants need water and sunlight so that they can grow.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your helper for a cup of water, then ask the tutor why water helps your body work well.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To rest with your eyes closed, usually at night.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I sleep well so that my body can grow.
practiceSentences: Idris falls asleep as soon as the light goes off.
What time do you sleep on a school night?
“A good sleep helps you learn,” said Doctor Sarah.
I sleep for ten hours and wake up full of energy.
Cats sleep in the sunny corner of the room.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, talk about your bedtime and ask the tutor why sleep helps children grow.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Good food gives me energy to run.
practiceSentences: Maya has lots of energy after a big breakfast.
Where does your energy come from each morning?
“Save some energy for the last race,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I have more energy on the days when I sleep early.
Porridge and fruit give runners the energy they need.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - e - r - g - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to help you list your breakfast, then ask the tutor which food gives the most energy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To put your hand or your finger onto something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Touch your toes ten times.
practiceSentences: Nora can touch the top of the door frame.
Can you touch your nose with your eyes closed?
“Touch your shoulders, then your knees,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I touch the cold glass and my finger tingles.
Please do not touch the wet paint on the wall.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With a grown-up nearby, give the tutor one touch command and ask it to check that your command is correct.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move your body around so that you face another way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Turn around, then stand still.
practiceSentences: Sami turns to face the window when he hears the bell.
Which way should we turn at the end of the path?
“Turn to your partner and say hello,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I turn my chair so that I can see the board.
The dancers turn slowly and then stop together.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to call out left or right while you turn, then ask the tutor to play the game again.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To be up on your feet, and not sitting down.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Stand on one foot for five seconds.
practiceSentences: Leo stands beside the door and waits for his class.
How long can you stand on one foot?
“Stand tall and take a deep breath,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I stand up when my grandmother Hana comes into the room.
The runners stand in a line before the race begins.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper timing you, stand on one foot and ask the tutor for one more balance command.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stretch your arm out to get to something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Reach for the sky with both hands.
practiceSentences: Amal can reach the shelf if she stands on her toes.
Can you reach the ball under the bench?
“Reach up, then reach down to your feet,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I reach for my water bottle after every race.
Tall sunflowers reach towards the warm sunshine.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to hold a soft toy high up, reach for it, and ask the tutor to describe what you did.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move your arms up and down like the wings of a bird.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Flap your arms like a bird.
practiceSentences: Mina flaps her arms and pretends to fly.
Can you flap your arms slowly and then quickly?
“Flap your arms four times,” said Nora in the game.
I flap my hands to cool my face.
Ducks flap their wings before they land on the water.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, flap your arms like a bird and ask the tutor to name another animal that flaps.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Identify and name different parts of your body.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Ten correct labels on the body diagram in Writing 1, and ten body parts named aloud in the Speaking 1 recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Understand and use action words about movement.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Every action word in the poem and the story identified, and the -ing action words used correctly in Activity 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Follow simple movement instructions in English.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct actions performed in the Leader Says game and in the poem read-along, with no more than one prompt.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give simple movement instructions to others.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Six verb-first commands written in Grammar 4 and delivered clearly in the Speaking 4 coaching recording.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe ways to stay healthy and strong.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five healthy habits written in Writing 3, and the True or False and sorting answers in Activities 4 and 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Create and follow a simple exercise routine.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A titled five-step routine written in Writing 6, then led for a family member and shown in the live showcase.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what someone is doing right now, using am / is / are + verb+ing.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct present continuous sentences in Writing 2, and five more in the Speaking 6 race report.

readings.title: A Poem: “Reach for the Sky!”
readings.passageScript:
Read the poem and do each action as you say it:
Reach for the sky!
Clap your hands, touch your toes.
Turn around. Put your finger on your nose.
Flap your arms, jump up high.
Wiggle your fingers and reach for the sky!

readings.title: Healthy and Strong
readings.passageScript: Walking, running and cycling are all great ways to keep fit and healthy. Moving your body every day helps you stay strong. Eating healthy food gives you energy to play and learn. Drinking water helps your body work well. Getting enough sleep helps you grow.

readings.title: “Get Up and Move” Day
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this text about a special day at school. Ask an adult to read it aloud. Then read it again by yourself.
“Today is ‘Get Up and Move’ day at our school. All the children are doing different activities. Some children are jumping and hopping. Others are waving their hands and nodding their heads. Everyone is having fun while moving their bodies. Moving helps us stay healthy and strong.”

readings.title: The Big Race
readings.genre: Realistic school story with lots of dialogue.
readings.theme: A school sports day — moving your body, doing your best, and helping your team.
readings.setting: The school field on sports day.
readings.passageScript:
It was sports day at Amal's school. The sun was warm and the field was full of children. Everyone was ready to run, jump and hop.
“Drink your water,” said Teacher Yasmin. “Move your bodies and do your best. A strong body is a healthy body!”
Amal was in the green team with Nora, Sami and Leo. Theo was in the red team. Theo could run very fast, and he wanted everyone to know.
“My legs are the fastest legs in the school,” said Theo. He waved his arms and jumped up high. “The red team will win!”
“Do not worry,” Nora said to her team. She nodded her head. “We will run together and do our best.”
The first race was the hopping race. The children lined up. Teacher Yasmin raised her hand.
“Ready, steady, go!” she called.
The children hopped on one foot across the field. Leo was hopping fast, but he wobbled and nearly fell. “Keep going, Leo!” shouted Amal, clapping her hands. Leo did not give up. He hopped all the way to the line.
Next came the big relay race. In a relay, the runners take turns. Each runner passes a stick to the next one. Amal was the last runner for the green team.
“Ready, steady, go!” called Teacher Yasmin again.
Nora ran fast. Sami ran faster. Then Leo passed the stick to Amal. But Amal's tummy felt full of butterflies. Her legs felt slow.
“I am running as fast as I can,” Amal puffed. “But Theo is winning!”
Theo was far in front. He was busy waving to the crowd. He did not look at his feet. Suddenly he tripped and fell onto the grass. His stick rolled away.
Amal ran past him. Her team was cheering. “Go, Amal, go!” they shouted, clapping and jumping. Amal reached the line first. The green team had won!
Everyone cheered and waved. But Amal looked back. Theo was still sitting on the grass. He was rubbing his knee, and he looked very sad.
Amal did not jump for joy. She walked back to Theo and put out her hand.
“Are you hurt?” she asked kindly.
“Only a little,” said Theo. “But I lost. And I said so many silly things.”
Amal smiled. “You are still the fastest runner in the school,” she said. “Come on. Stand up.”
She helped Theo stand. They walked back together, and all the children clapped for them.
Teacher Yasmin nodded and smiled. “Well done, everyone,” she said. “Amal, you ran your best and you never gave up. And you were kind to Theo. That is the true winner.”
That evening, Amal told her mother about the big race. “I won,” she said, “but the best part was helping Theo. Next time, we will be on the same team!”
Her mother hugged her. “You have a fast body and a kind heart,” she said. “Now drink your water and rest. Tomorrow is another day to move and play.”

comprehension.question: Which action words does the poem tell you to do? List them all.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Reach, clap, touch, turn, put, flap, jump and wiggle.
comprehension.explanation: The poem gives eight commands in order, one or two in every line. Reading each line slowly helps you find them all.

comprehension.question: Which body parts does the poem name? List them all.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Hands, toes, finger, nose, arms and fingers.
comprehension.explanation: Hands and toes are in line two, finger and nose in line three, arms in line four, and fingers in the last line.

comprehension.question: Which line of the poem is said at the beginning and then again at the end?
comprehension.explanation: Poems often repeat a line. Here the poem opens with “Reach for the sky!” and the last line finishes with the same words.

comprehension.question: Which three activities does the text name as great ways to keep fit and healthy?
comprehension.explanation: The very first sentence of the text lists all three activities together.

comprehension.question: According to the text, what does moving your body every day help you to do?
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence says that moving your body every day helps you stay strong.

comprehension.question: The text says that one thing gives you energy to play and learn. What is it?
comprehension.explanation: The text links energy to food, not to water or sleep, which the other sentences link to different benefits.

comprehension.question: The text mentions water and sleep. What does each one do for your body?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Drinking water helps your body work well, and getting enough sleep helps you grow.
comprehension.explanation: Two separate sentences give the two answers, so a full answer names both.

comprehension.question: What is the special day at school called in this listening text?
comprehension.explanation: The first sentence of the spoken text names the day.

comprehension.question: Name at least three movements that the children in the text are doing.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any three of: jumping, hopping, waving their hands, nodding their heads.
comprehension.explanation: The text names four movements, so a child who lists three of them has answered fully.

comprehension.question: The text ends by saying why moving matters. What does moving help us to be?
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence of the text says that moving helps us stay healthy and strong.

comprehension.question: What special day is it at Amal's school?
comprehension.explanation: The story opens by telling you that it was sports day at Amal's school.

comprehension.question: Which team is Amal in, and who runs for the red team?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Amal is in the green team with Nora, Sami and Leo. Theo runs for the red team.
comprehension.explanation: The third paragraph names the green team, and the next sentence says that Theo was in the red team.

comprehension.question: What did Amal shout to Leo during the hopping race?
comprehension.explanation: When Leo wobbled and nearly fell, Amal clapped her hands and called out to encourage him.

comprehension.question: Why did Theo trip and fall in the relay race?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He was waving to the crowd, so he did not look at his feet.
comprehension.explanation: The story says Theo was so busy waving that he did not watch where he was running, and then he tripped.

comprehension.question: What kind thing did Amal do after she won, and why do you think she did it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She walked back and helped Theo stand up. Accept any reason that uses the story, such as Theo looking sad or rubbing his hurt knee.
comprehension.explanation: Amal saw Theo sitting on the grass and put out her hand instead of celebrating. Look for evidence from the story in the reason.

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel when her legs felt slow, and what did she do instead of giving up?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her tummy felt full of butterflies and she felt worried, but she kept running as fast as she could.
comprehension.explanation: The butterflies in her tummy show her nerves, and her own words show that she carried on running.

comprehension.question: Teacher Yasmin said that being kind to Theo made Amal “the true winner”. What do you think she meant?
comprehension.correctAnswer: That kindness and effort matter more than crossing the line first.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin praised two things: Amal never gave up, and she helped Theo. A strong answer names both.

comprehension.question: Amal kept running even when her legs felt slow. Tell about a time when you kept trying at something hard. What happened?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept any personal example that names the hard thing, says what the child did to keep going, and says how it ended.
comprehension.explanation: This question links the story to the child's own life. Ask them to compare their effort with Amal's in the relay race.

grammar.title: Giving Commands (Imperatives)
grammar.explanation: A command tells someone what to do. We use commands to give movement instructions in a game, in an exercise or at a race. Learning commands helps you follow your teacher and lead an action game yourself.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A command starts with the verb, and you do not need to say “you” first.
Touch your toes.
Clap your hands.
Jump up high.
Stand on one foot.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “You touching your toes.” The action word needs to come first on its own, so the command is “Touch your toes.”
grammar.memoryTip: A command is short and busy. It jumps straight to the action word: “Jump!” “Clap!” “Stand!”
grammar.practice:
Write the command for each picture. Use a verb and a body part.
1. ______ your ______. (someone touching their toes)
2. ______ your ______. (someone clapping their hands)
3. ______ up ______. (someone jumping high)
4. ______ on one ______. (someone standing on one foot)

grammar.title: Giving Commands: Lead the Warm-Up
grammar.explanation: Now you give the commands yourself. A good warm-up leader says one clear command at a time, and always begins with the action word so that the team knows exactly what to do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Choose an action word, then say what moves and how much.
Reach for the sky.
Turn around slowly.
Flap your arms like a bird.
Wiggle your fingers ten times.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to slip back into a describing sentence, such as “We are turning around.” That tells what people do; a command tells them to do it, so write “Turn around.”
grammar.memoryTip: Ask yourself, “Am I telling or am I ordering?” If you are ordering, the action word goes first every time.
grammar.practice: Write six commands for your own two-minute warm-up. Use six different action words taken from Group 2 and Group 4 of your word list. Then read your warm-up aloud and lead your family through it.

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now (Present Continuous)
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about an action that is happening right now. It helps you say what you and other people are doing at this very moment.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use subject + am / is / are + verb with -ing.
Use am with I: “I am jumping.”
Use is with he, she and it: “She is hopping.” “He is clapping.”
Use are with you, we and they: “They are waving.” “We are nodding.”
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “She jumping.” leaves out the helping word. Put is in front of the -ing word and write “She is jumping.”
grammar.memoryTip: Say the little rhyme: “I am, he, she, it is, you, we, they are, and never forget the -ing!”
grammar.practice:
Choose am, is or are for each sentence.
1. I ____ clapping my hands.
2. He ____ touching his toes.
3. They ____ waving their arms.
4. We ____ hopping on one foot.
5. She ____ nodding her head.

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now: Report the Race
grammar.explanation: A sports reporter describes what is happening while it happens. In this task you use am, is and are with -ing words to report the action of a race in real time.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Match the helping word to the person before you add the -ing verb.
I am running to the line.
Theo is waving to the crowd.
Nora and Sami are cheering loudly.
We are clapping for both teams.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often use is for more than one person, as in “Nora and Sami is cheering.” Two people need are, so the sentence becomes “Nora and Sami are cheering.”
grammar.memoryTip: Count the people first. One person takes is, and two or more people take are.
grammar.practice: Look again at the relay race in “The Big Race”. Write five sentences that report the action as it happens. Use I am once, he is or she is twice, and they are twice.

grammar.title: Making the -ing Form
grammar.explanation: To say that an action is happening now, we add -ing to the verb. Most verbs are easy, but a few verbs change their spelling first. Getting this right keeps your writing neat and correct.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs: just add -ing. jump becomes jumping, and touch becomes touching.
Verbs ending in a silent e: drop the e, then add -ing. wave becomes waving, and wiggle becomes wiggling.
Short verbs with one vowel and one final consonant: double the last letter, then add -ing. hop becomes hopping, and clap becomes clapping.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “She is waveing.” keeps the silent e, so drop the e and write “She is waving.” In the same way, “He is hoping on one foot.” needs a double p to become “He is hopping.”
grammar.memoryTip: Silent e says goodbye before -ing, as in wave and waving. A short word with one vowel doubles its last letter for company, as in hop and hopping.
grammar.practice:
Write the -ing form of each verb.
1. wave → __________
2. hop → __________
3. clap → __________
4. wiggle → __________
5. touch → __________

grammar.title: Making the -ing Form: Spelling Detective
grammar.explanation: A spelling detective decides which of the three -ing rules a verb needs before writing it down. In this task you sort verbs into the three groups and then use them in your own sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Ask three questions about the verb.
Does it end in a silent e? Then drop the e: exercise becomes exercising.
Is it short, with one vowel and one final consonant? Then double the letter: nod becomes nodding.
If neither is true, just add -ing: reach becomes reaching.
grammar.commonMistake: A longer word such as listen does not double its last letter, so listenning is not right. Only short words with one vowel, such as hop and nod, double the final letter.
grammar.memoryTip: Three doors: drop the e, double the letter, or walk straight through and add -ing.
grammar.practice: Sort these ten verbs into the three -ing groups, then write the -ing form of each one: jump, wave, hop, touch, wiggle, nod, reach, clap, stand, exercise. Finish by writing three sentences, each using one verb from a different group.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Naming Body Parts
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Stand in front of a mirror, or point to your own body while an adult watches.
Say “This is my ______.” for one part (head, arm, hand, tummy).
Say “These are my ______.” for more than one part (fingers, toes).
Name at least ten body parts, then record yourself. Check: did you say each word clearly, and did you choose “This is” or “These are” correctly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name a body part while your helper listens, then point to it and say the whole sentence back.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Describing Actions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Do an action, and while you are still doing it say what is happening.
Say “I am ______.” about yourself (jumping, clapping, waving).
Ask a family member to move, then say “He is ______.” or “She is ______.”
Watch two people move together and say “They are ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Do one action, describe it out loud, and ask the tutor whether you chose am, is or are correctly. Keep an adult in the room.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Leader Says
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this movement command game with an adult or a friend.
One person is the leader and gives a command, such as “The leader says, touch your toes!”
Only do the action when the leader says “The leader says” first.
If the leader only says “Jump!”, stay perfectly still. Swap roles after five commands.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to be the leader for ten commands while your adult helper plays beside you, then take a turn as leader yourself.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Movement Coach
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be the coach for a two-minute warm-up.
Give six clear commands in order, one at a time, such as “Stand up straight.” and “Reach for the sky.”
Speak slowly and loudly enough for your team to hear every word.
Record your warm-up and listen back. Did every command begin with an action word?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your six commands to the tutor and ask it to say which one was the clearest and why. An adult should sit with you.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Healthy Habit Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Interview someone at home about staying healthy and strong.
Ask these three questions: “What exercise do you enjoy?”, “How much water do you drink each day?” and “What time do you sleep?”
Listen carefully and say one thing back that you heard.
Record your interview, then report two of the answers to your class.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Practise your three interview questions with the tutor first, with an adult beside you, and ask it to suggest one extra question about healthy food.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Sports Reporter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Report a race as if it is happening right now.
Describe “The Big Race” from the moment Amal takes the stick to the moment she reaches the line.
Use at least five sentences with am, is or are and an -ing word, such as “Theo is waving to the crowd.”
Record your report and play it to your family.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to listen to your race report and to name every -ing verb it hears. Have an adult nearby while you record.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Label Your Body Parts
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw a simple person on a large sheet of paper. Label at least ten body parts, using one line for each label. Then write one sentence about four of your labels, using the frame “I use my ______ to ______.”
writing.modelText:
Labels: head, eyes, ears, nose, mouth, arms, hands, fingers, legs, feet.
I use my hands to clap. I use my legs to run. I use my eyes to read. I use my fingers to hold my pencil.
writing.successCriteria: I labelled ten body parts, spelled each label correctly, wrote four full sentences, and used a capital letter and a full stop in every sentence.
writing.support: Copy the labels from your word list first. Say each sentence aloud before you write it.
writing.extension: Add two more sentences that use because, such as “I use my legs to run because running keeps me strong.”

writing.title: Writing 2 — Describing Actions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write five sentences about what people are doing right now. Use am, is or are with a verb ending in -ing. Write about one person in three sentences, and about two or more people in the other two.
writing.modelText: The boy is jumping up and down. The girl is waving her hand. I am hopping on one foot. The children are clapping their hands. We are nodding our heads to the beat.
writing.successCriteria: Each sentence has a helping word (am, is or are), a verb ending in -ing, a capital letter and a full stop.
writing.support: Say the am, is and are rhyme from Grammar Lesson 2 before you begin writing.
writing.extension: Add a where or when detail to two of your sentences, such as “on the school field” or “after lunch”.

writing.title: Writing 3 — My Healthy Habits List
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a list of at least five healthy habits that you can do this week. Number each habit, write it as a full sentence, and draw a small picture beside it.
writing.modelText:
1. I drink water every day.
2. I eat fruit and vegetables.
3. I play outside and move my body.
4. I sleep early on school nights.
5. I wash my hands before I eat.
writing.successCriteria: My list has five numbered habits, and each one is a full sentence that begins with a capital letter and ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Look back at the reading “Healthy and Strong” for ideas you can borrow.
writing.extension: Tick each habit every evening for one week, then write one sentence about how the week felt.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Story Response: The Big Race
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer three tasks about “The Big Race”. First write three sentences about a game or sport that you enjoy, using action words. Then write two kind things you could say to a friend who has just lost a race. Finally draw Amal helping Theo to stand up, and write one sentence under your picture.
writing.modelText:
I am playing football with my cousin. I am running fast across the grass. I am jumping to reach the ball.
You tried so hard today. Come and play with me next time.
Amal is helping Theo to stand up after his fall.
writing.successCriteria: I used action words in my sport sentences, wrote two kind sentences for a friend, and wrote one clear caption for my picture.
writing.support: Reread the last four paragraphs of the story before you write the kind words.
writing.extension: Write what Theo might say back to Amal on the following morning.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Exercise Routine
writing.promptAndInstructions: Design your own five-step exercise routine and write it as a set of commands. Give your routine a title, number each step, and say how many times to do it.
writing.modelText:
My Wake-Up Routine
1. Stand on one foot for five seconds.
2. Reach for the sky ten times.
3. Touch your toes five times.
4. Hop on the spot ten times.
5. Flap your arms like a bird for ten seconds.
writing.successCriteria: Every step begins with an action word, tells how many times to move, and ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Choose your action words from Group 2 and Group 4 of your word list.
writing.extension: Teach your routine to a family member, then write one sentence about how it went.

writing.title: Writing 5 — A Healthy Day
writing.promptAndInstructions: Plan one healthy day from morning to bedtime. Write six sentences in order, one for each part of the day: waking up, breakfast, before school, lunch, afternoon and bedtime. Begin each sentence with a time phrase.
writing.modelText: In the morning I drink a glass of water. At breakfast I eat porridge and fruit. Before school I walk with my sister Mina. At lunch I eat rice, beans and salad. In the afternoon I play outside for an hour. At bedtime I sleep for ten hours.
writing.successCriteria: My six sentences are in time order, each one opens with a time phrase, and each one names a healthy choice.
writing.support: Use these time phrases: in the morning, at breakfast, before school, at lunch, in the afternoon, at bedtime.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence that explains why one of your choices keeps you strong.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Match the Body Part to an Action
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each body part with an action you can do with it. Draw a line between the pairs, or write them out.
Body parts: hands, head, arms, fingers, toes, legs
Actions: clap, nod, wave, wiggle, tap, jump
activities.answerSummary: hands → clap, head → nod, arms → wave, fingers → wiggle, toes → tap, legs → jump

activities.title: Activity 2 — Fill in the Blanks (Body Parts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write out each sentence in full and fill the gap with the correct body part.
1. I use my ______ to see.
2. I use my ______ to hear.
3. I use my ______ to walk.
4. I use my ______ to hold things.
5. I use my ______ to smell.
activities.answerSummary: 1. eyes, 2. ears, 3. legs (feet is also correct), 4. hands, 5. nose

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks (Action Words)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct -ing form of the verb in brackets.
1. I am ______ (jump) up and down.
2. She is ______ (clap) her hands.
3. He is ______ (touch) his toes.
4. They are ______ (wave) their arms.
5. We are ______ (hop) on one foot.

activities.title: Activity 4 — True or False (Healthy Habits)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each statement. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. Drinking water helps keep your body healthy.
2. Eating only sweets gives you energy to learn.
3. Moving your body every day helps you stay strong.
4. Watching television all day is good exercise.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, because fruit, vegetables and other healthy food give you energy. 3. True. 4. False, because active play and exercise keep your body strong.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Build a Movement Sequence
activities.instructionsAndItems: Write six action cards, one word on each card: hop, jump, clap, wave, turn, stand. Shuffle the cards and lay four of them in a row. Read the row aloud as commands and do the actions in order. Then write those four commands as full sentences.
activities.answerSummary: Any four cards in any order are accepted. Each written command must begin with the action word and a capital letter, such as “Hop three times.” and “Turn around.”

activities.title: Activity 6 — Healthy Choice Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort these eight items into two columns headed Helps my body and Does not help my body: water, sweets all day, ten hours of sleep, sitting still all afternoon, fruit, running outside, staying awake late, vegetables.
activities.answerSummary: Helps my body: water, ten hours of sleep, fruit, running outside, vegetables. Does not help my body: sweets all day, sitting still all afternoon, staying awake late.

assignments.title: Ready, Steady, Go! movement and health portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work. First complete “Writing 6 — My Exercise Routine” and write your five commands neatly. Next record “Speaking 4 — Movement Coach”, leading your own routine out loud. Finally complete “Writing 3 — My Healthy Habits List”. Check all three against the writing and speaking rubrics before you submit them together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: Which word means the part at the end of your arm, with fingers?
quizzes.explanation: A hand sits at the end of each arm. Water, sleep and energy are health words, not body parts.

quizzes.question: Sami holds his pencil with his ____s.
quizzes.explanation: We hold a pencil between our fingers and thumb. Toes are on your feet, and water and turn are not body parts of the hand.

quizzes.question: Which word means to jump up and down on one foot?
quizzes.explanation: Hopping uses one foot only. Clapping uses hands, water is a drink, and head is a body part.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the present continuous correctly?
quizzes.options: She jumping on the grass. | She are jumping on the grass. | She am jumping on the grass. | She is jumping on the grass.
quizzes.correctAnswer: She is jumping on the grass.
quizzes.explanation: She takes the helping word is. Am belongs with I, and are belongs with you, we and they.

quizzes.question: Which is the correct -ing form of the verb hop?
quizzes.explanation: Hop is a short word with one vowel, so the p doubles before -ing. Hoping comes from hope, and hopped is a past form.

quizzes.question: Which of these sentences is a command?
quizzes.options: Touch your toes. | My toes are cold. | Are your toes cold? | I am touching my toes.
quizzes.explanation: A command starts with the action word and tells someone to do something. The others describe, ask or report.

quizzes.question: Which of these helps your body stay healthy and strong?
quizzes.options: Watching television all day | Sitting still for many hours | Staying awake all night | Drinking water every day
quizzes.explanation: Water helps your body work well. The other three choices keep your body still or tired.

quizzes.question: In the poem “Reach for the Sky!”, where do you put your finger?
quizzes.options: On your knee | On your ear | On your nose | On your elbow
quizzes.explanation: Line three of the poem says, “Put your finger on your nose.” The other body parts are not in the poem.

quizzes.question: In “The Big Race”, why did Theo fall over?
quizzes.options: He was waving to the crowd and did not watch his feet. | He tripped over a big bag that somebody had left on the grass. | He stopped in the middle of the race to drink his water. | He was helping Amal to stand up again after her fall.
quizzes.correctAnswer: He was waving to the crowd and did not watch his feet.
quizzes.explanation: The story says Theo was so busy waving that he did not look at his feet, and then he tripped.

quizzes.question: What does the word energy mean?
quizzes.options: One of the small parts at the end of your foot. | To move your head up and down to say yes. | The power you get from food and rest to play and learn. | To stretch your arm out to something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.
quizzes.explanation: Energy is what food and rest give you. The other three options describe a toe, nodding and reaching.

liveSessions.title: Ready, Steady, Go!: body parts and action words
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn Group 1 of the word list: head, arm, hand, finger, tummy and toe.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and gentle warm-up; 10 min body-part retrieval on a labelled diagram; 10 min action-word mime game; 15 min Speaking 1 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Naming Body Parts and upload it.

liveSessions.title: Shared reading: The Big Race
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read “The Big Race” once at home and underline every action word that you meet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min story recap; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 11 to 15; 10 min hot-seating Amal and Theo
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer the three Think and talk questions in your book.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: giving commands
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar Lesson 1 and try Practice 1 on your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min command warm-up; 10 min the verb-first rule with examples; 15 min Leader Says game; 10 min writing six commands; 5 min sharing
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Grammar 4 and draft the commands for your warm-up.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

liveSessions.title: Grammar and speaking: actions happening now
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar Lesson 2 and learn the am, is and are rhyme by heart.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min rhyme chant; 15 min sorting am / is / are with mime; 15 min modelling the sports reporter; 10 min paired reporting
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

liveSessions.title: Guided writing: healthy days and healthy habits
liveSessions.beforeSession: Reread “Healthy and Strong” and jot down three healthy habits of your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min habits share; 10 min time-phrase openers; 20 min guided writing of A Healthy Day; 10 min partner proofreading
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 5 — A Healthy Day and check every capital letter.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

liveSessions.title: Ready, Steady, Go!: routine showcase and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise the exercise routine that you designed in Writing 6.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome; 20 min routine showcase with every child leading once; 10 min checkpoint review; 10 min self-assessment
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. Prompts drawn from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: “Can you help me learn more body part words?”, “What is the difference between hop and jump?”, “Why is drinking water important?”, “Can you give me a command to follow?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “Why do we say hopping with two letter p's but jumping with one?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise am, is and are?” Reading and story: “Can you read the poem with me and help me do the actions?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Writing: “Can you check if my action sentences are correct?”, “What other healthy habits can I add to my list?”

This is an active unit, so clear a safe space before every movement task and check that the floor is dry and shoes are on. Children with limited mobility can do every action with hands, head and fingers, or can lead as the caller in Leader Says. Keep water bottles nearby and pause for a drink after each burst of movement. When marking, treat a missing helping word (am, is or are) as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise the child for choosing the right -ing spelling. The story carries the unit's value: keep trying, and be kind when you win.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: hands → clap, head → nod, arms → wave, fingers → wiggle, toes → tap, legs → jump.
1. eyes, 2. ears, 3. legs (accept feet), 4. hands, 5. nose. The sentence must be copied out in full.
1. jumping, 2. clapping (double the p), 3. touching, 4. waving (drop the e), 5. hopping (double the p).
1. True. 2. False, healthy food such as fruit and vegetables gives you energy. 3. True. 4. False, active play is exercise and watching television is not.
1. Touch your toes. 2. Clap your hands. 3. Jump up high. 4. Stand on one foot. Each answer must begin with the action word and end with a full stop.
1. am, 2. is, 3. are, 4. are, 5. is.
1. waving, 2. hopping, 3. clapping, 4. wiggling, 5. touching.
Reach, clap, touch, turn, put, flap, jump, wiggle. Award the mark for six or more of the eight actions.
Hands, toes, finger, nose, arms and fingers, which is six body parts in total.
“Reach for the sky!” It opens the poem and returns in the final line, which makes it the repeated line.
Walking, running and cycling. All three are needed for the full mark.
It helps you stay strong. Accept wording such as it keeps my body strong.
Eating healthy food. Do not accept water or sleep, which the text links to other benefits.
Water helps your body work well, and sleep helps you grow. Both halves are needed.
“Get Up and Move” day. Accept the words with or without capital letters.
Any three of jumping, hopping, waving hands and nodding heads.
Healthy and strong. Accept one of the two words with the idea of keeping the body well.
It is sports day. Accept sports day at school.
Green team: Amal with Nora, Sami and Leo. Red team: Theo.
“Keep going, Leo!” Accept the words without the exclamation mark.
He was waving to the crowd and did not look at his feet, so he tripped on the grass.
She walked back and helped Theo to stand up. Accept any reason grounded in Theo's sad face or his hurt knee.
Her tummy felt full of butterflies and her legs felt slow, but she kept running as fast as she could.
Kindness and effort matter more than crossing the line first. Look for both ideas: she never gave up, and she helped Theo.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names the hard task, one thing they did to keep going, and the result.
hand. The other three options are health words rather than body parts.
finger. We hold a pencil between the fingers and the thumb.
hop. Hopping uses one foot, while jumping uses both feet.
She is jumping on the grass. The word she always takes is.
hopping. Hop is short with one vowel, so the p doubles before -ing.
Ten correct labels spelled as on the word list, plus four sentences built on the frame I use my ______ to ______.
Six commands, each opening with a different action word from the word list, for example Reach for the sky. and Turn around slowly. Reject any sentence that starts with a subject.
Five report sentences: one with I am, two with he is or she is, and two with they are. Every verb must carry -ing.
Just add -ing: jump, touch, reach, stand. Drop the e: wave, wiggle, exercise. Double the last letter: hop, nod, clap. Then three sentences, one verb from each group.
Listen for six commands given one at a time, each beginning with the action word and loud enough to follow. Mark clarity and order, not the difficulty of the exercises.
The recording must contain all three interview questions, one repeat-back of something heard, and two answers reported afterwards.
Count the present continuous sentences: five or more, each with the right helping word. The report must run from the stick pass to the finish line.
Three action sentences about the child's own sport, two kind sentences for a friend who lost, and one caption naming Amal and Theo.
Five numbered commands under a title, each with the action word first and a number of repeats, each ending with a full stop.
Six sentences in time order, each opening with a time phrase and naming one healthy choice.
Any four of the six cards in any order. Check that each written command starts with the action word and a capital letter.
Helps my body: water, ten hours of sleep, fruit, running outside, vegetables. Does not help my body: sweets all day, sitting still all afternoon, staying awake late.
Touch your toes. It is the only option that gives an order; the others describe, ask or report.
Drinking water every day. The other three choices leave the body still or tired.
On your nose. Line three of the poem says to put your finger on your nose.
He was waving to the crowd and did not watch his feet.
The power you get from food and rest to play and learn.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name different parts of my body.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use action words about movement.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can follow simple movement instructions in English.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give simple movement instructions to other people.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe ways to stay healthy and strong.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what someone is doing now, using am, is or are with an -ing word.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and easy to follow
Most body and action words used correctly
rubrics.level4: Wide range of unit words used naturally

rubrics.level2: Helping word used after a reminder
rubrics.level3: am, is and are mostly correct with -ing
rubrics.level4: am, is and are always correct with -ing

rubrics.level1: Needs full support to take a turn
rubrics.level4: Takes turns, listens and adds a new idea

rubrics.level1: Ideas unclear or away from the task
rubrics.level4: Clear ideas with well chosen detail

rubrics.level2: Some order, with gaps in the sequence
rubrics.level4: Strong order using time phrases or numbered steps

rubrics.level3: Good use of body, action and health words
rubrics.level4: Precise word choices that suit the task

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops mostly missing
rubrics.level2: Some capital letters and full stops
rubrics.level3: Capital letters, spaces and full stops usually correct
